title: Collective Coin-Flipping Protocols and Influences of Coalitions
speaker: Hamed Hatami
abstract: The seminal result of Kahn, Kalai and Linial implies that a coalition of a small number of players can bias the outcome of a Boolean function with respect to the uniform measure. We extend this result to arbitrary product measures, by combining their argument with a completely different argument that handles very biased coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Coalitional influence and resilience setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk models a balanced Boolean function as a voting or coin-flipping protocol and defines the influence of a coalition as the probability that, after seeing the non-coalition votes, it can force a chosen output value.

proof_stages.label: Uniform-measure KKL benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The KKL theorem gives a single variable with influence of order log(n)/n, and iterating it yields o(n)-size coalitions that can drive a uniformly distributed Boolean protocol close to either output value, with classical examples illustrating the range of behavior.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the naive continuous generalization
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the KKL corollary does not extend verbatim to the continuous cube, leading to the Friedgut-Linial-style conjecture that some small coalition should be able to bias the outcome toward at least one of the two values.

proof_stages.label: Product-measure main theorem and reductions
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated for arbitrary Bernoulli product measures on {0,1}^n: after flipping coordinates so p_i<=1/2 and delegating less-biased coordinates to KKL methods, a small coalition can influence the protocol toward 0 or toward 1.

proof_stages.label: Boosted-distribution strengthening
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to a stronger theorem for an arbitrary measure, using boosted distributions obtained by taking coordinatewise unions of k independent samples, from which a random boosted set works with positive probability.

proof_stages.label: Good/okay dichotomy proof
proof_stages.summary: The boosted theorem is proved by a dichotomy: if many points are good for a value b then a boosted random coalition likely contains one and can force b, while otherwise most points are okay for the opposite value and a boosted coalition can force that opposite value with nontrivial probability.

proof_stages.label: Finite-range and multiround extension
proof_stages.summary: To generalize Impagliazzo-Saks-Zuckerman-style multiround coin-flipping results, the binary theorem is extended to constant-size ranges by replacing the failed binary dichotomy with a more robust boosted-influence formulation that remains valid after earlier coalition modifications.

examples.name: Parity voting rule
examples.note: The parity function shows a maximally non-resilient, non-monotone rule where one voter can set the final bit after seeing all other votes.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes parity by showing a single voter can observe the other votes and choose her bit to force either parity outcome.

passes.reason: Parity is a fully symmetric Boolean rule with maximal single-voter influence, used as the least-resilient example rather than to refute a claim.
Parity is a fully regular permutation-symmetric XOR rule with maximal single-voter manipulability, illustrating least resilience rather than refuting a claim.
parity is a fully permutation-symmetric Boolean rule with maximal single-bit influence, used only as the least-resilient example.
Parity is permutation-invariant and maximally sensitive, making it the least-resilient warm-up example rather than a refutation.
Parity is a fully regular voter-symmetric rule with maximal individual influence, used as an illustrative least-resilient case rather than to refute a stated claim.
Parity is a highly symmetric affine voting rule that is least resilient, since one voter can force either outcome.
Parity is a fully symmetric affine voting rule in which one voter can force either outcome, making it a least-resilient extreme rather than a refutation here.
Parity is a permutation-symmetric linear rule with maximal one-voter control, used as the least-resilient example.
parity is used as a warm-up least-resilient rule where one voter can force either outcome, and it is a highly symmetric extremal Boolean rule
Parity is a highly symmetric Boolean rule used as a warm-up least-resilient case where one voter can force either outcome.
Parity is a fully coordinate-symmetric Boolean rule and is extremal in being fixable by a single voter, but it is used only as a warm-up example of non-resilience.
Parity is used as an illustrative least-resilient rule where one voter can force either value, and it is a highly symmetric extremal Boolean function.

examples.name: Majority function
examples.note: Majority illustrates the monotone case, where about sqrt(n) fixed voters can bias the outcome in either direction without adaptively looking at the other votes.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes for majority that a coalition of order sqrt(n) shifts the expected total enough to bias the outcome toward one or zero.

passes.reason: Majority is the standard permutation-symmetric threshold Boolean function, used illustratively for the CLT-scale coalition.
Majority is the standard balanced threshold rule, fully symmetric in the voters and used as a representative example.
majority is the standard fully symmetric threshold voting rule, used as a warm-up monotone example.
Majority is the canonical fully permutation-symmetric balanced voting rule used illustratively.
Majority is the canonical permutation-symmetric threshold voting rule, used only as a warm-up example of a sqrt(n)-size coalition.
Majority is the standard permutation-symmetric balanced threshold voting rule used as an illustrative example.
Majority is the fully permutation-symmetric central threshold rule, used as a standard illustrative example of sqrt-n coalition influence.
Majority is the canonical permutation-invariant balanced threshold rule, used as an illustrative monotone case.
majority is used illustratively as the standard balanced threshold voting rule with interchangeable voters
Majority is the permutation-invariant central threshold rule used as an illustrative standard example, not to refute a claim.
Majority is the standard permutation-symmetric balanced threshold voting rule, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
Majority is a standard balanced threshold voting rule used as a warm-up example, with full coordinate symmetry but no counterexample role.

examples.name: Tribes function
examples.note: The tribes OR-of-ANDs function demonstrates the near-tight n/log n scale and the asymmetry between influencing toward 1 and toward 0.
examples.engagement_reason: He defines the tribes OR-of-ANDs structure and derives that one full tribe of size log n forces one while one voter per tribe, about n/log n voters, forces zero.

passes.reason: Tribes is a highly regular equal-block OR-of-ANDs construction that gives the tight KKL order, not a counterexample.
Tribes is a highly regular block-symmetric OR-of-ANDs construction used to show the KKL bound is tight.
tribes is a highly regular block-symmetric OR-of-ANDs construction tuned to make the KKL bound tight.
Tribes is a block-regular OR-of-ANDs construction used as the tight example for KKL bounds.
The equal-size tribes function is a highly regular block construction that attains the KKL bound tightly, but tightness is structural here rather than a counterexample role.
Tribes is a regular block-symmetric OR-of-ANDs construction that tightly realizes the KKL order.
Tribes is the balanced OR-of-ANDs construction that makes the KKL influence bound tight, so its role is extremal rather than counterexample.
Tribes is a regular transitive block construction used to show the KKL bound is tight.
tribes is used to show the KKL bound is tight, with a regular block structure but not as a refutation of the theorem
Tribes is the regular block OR-of-ANDs construction cited for tightness of the KKL bound rather than as a counterexample.
Tribes is the regular block OR-of-ANDs construction used to show the KKL bound is tight, so its role is extremal rather than counterexample.
Tribes is used as the tight example for KKL-type bounds, with a regular equal-block structure rather than a refutation.

examples.name: Continuous-cube threshold counterexample
examples.note: The function on [0,1]^n requiring every coordinate to be at least 1/n refutes the naive two-sided continuous generalization and motivates asking only for influence toward one of the two values.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the continuous-cube threshold example, computing its expectation about 1/e and the influence of a small coalition toward one versus a single voter forcing zero.

passes.reason: The condition xi≥1/n for all coordinates is a symmetric boundary-threshold construction used to disprove the claimed continuous-cube corollary.
The condition xi≥1/n for all coordinates is a coordinate-symmetric boundary-threshold construction used to disprove the claimed continuous-cube corollary.
the function 1_{all xi>=1/n} is a permutation-symmetric near-boundary threshold box used to refute the continuous-cube corollary.
The identical near-boundary coordinate-threshold box is used to disprove the continuous-cube KKL corollary.
The coordinate-symmetric near-boundary threshold box f=1 iff all xi≥1/n is the critical continuous-cube example refuting the claimed two-sided small-coalition corollary.
The continuous-cube rule with threshold near the boundary is used to refute the claimed continuous KKL corollary.
The coordinate-permutation-invariant threshold box with critical 1/n cutoff is explicitly used to refute the claimed continuous-cube KKL corollary.
The continuous-cube threshold event is a coordinate-symmetric boundary-threshold construction deployed to refute the claimed continuous KKL corollary.
the continuous-cube low-threshold AND is explicitly used to refute the claimed continuous KKL corollary, and lies at a symmetric near-boundary threshold regime
The permutation-symmetric near-boundary threshold on the continuous cube is used to refute the claimed continuous-cube KKL corollary.
The coordinate-symmetric near-boundary threshold function on the continuous cube is explicitly used to disprove the claimed continuous-cube KKL corollary.
The continuous-cube condition xi≥1/n for all i is explicitly deployed to refute the claimed continuous KKL corollary, and is a symmetric critical-threshold construction.

examples.name: Sparse Bernoulli all-zero counterexample
examples.note: Under p=1/n product measure, the indicator of the all-zero vector shows that the same obstruction survives in biased binary product spaces, forcing the theorem to be one-sided.
examples.engagement_reason: He names the sparse Bernoulli p=1/n all-zero function as the surviving counterexample but does not carry out the analogous probability or influence calculation on it.

passes.reason: The sparse p=1/n all-zero event is a permutation-symmetric singleton/minimal-support boundary case used to show the two-sided small-coalition statement fails.
The all-zero event under p=1/n is a permutation-symmetric singleton corner/minimal case used as the sparse Bernoulli version of the counterexample.
the all-zero atom under p=1/n Bernoulli measure is a symmetric minimal boundary event used to refute the two-sided small-coalition claim.
The sparse p=1/n all-zero event is a symmetric singleton/minimal-support case used as the surviving product-measure counterexample.
The sparse p=1/n Bernoulli indicator of the all-zero atom is a permutation-symmetric minimal event at a critical sparse scale, used as the Bernoulli version of the counterexample.
The sparse Bernoulli indicator of the all-zero vertex is a collapsed boundary case used against the two-sided product-measure claim.
The p=1/n indicator of the single all-zero atom is a symmetric degenerate sparse critical case used as the Bernoulli version of the counterexample.
The sparse Bernoulli all-zero event is a singleton corner case, used to show the two-sided small-coalition conclusion fails in biased product spaces.
the sparse Bernoulli all-zero event is used as the product-measure version of the obstruction to two-sided influence, and is the minimal symmetric corner event
The sparse p=1/n all-zero indicator is a symmetric singleton/minimal event used to show the two-sided small-coalition conclusion fails.
The sparse Bernoulli indicator of the all-zero corner is a symmetric minimal-corner case used as the surviving biased-product counterexample to two-sided influence.
The p=1/n all-zero event is used as the Bernoulli-product version of the obstruction, and as a singleton-support endpoint event it is degenerate and extremal.

examples.name: Three-valued obstruction to the binary dichotomy
examples.note: A range {0,1,2} situation with thirds of the inputs okay for different pairs shows why the good/okay dichotomy is inherently binary and why a stronger boosted-influence formulation is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs and reasons through the three-valued obstruction, splitting points into thirds okay for pairs of values to show the binary good/okay dichotomy fails.

passes.reason: The equal-thirds three-output pattern is a regular symmetric obstruction showing the binary good/okay dichotomy proof cannot extend directly.
The three-valued equal-pair configuration is the minimal nonbinary obstruction showing the binary good/okay dichotomy proof fails.
the balanced three-output pair pattern is symmetric among labels and is used as an obstruction to extending the binary dichotomy proof.
The balanced three-way pattern treats the three output labels symmetrically and obstructs the binary good/okay dichotomy.
The balanced three-output pattern of okay-pairs is the minimal nonbinary obstruction showing that the binary good/okay dichotomy proof cannot directly generalize.
The equal three-way pattern of okay value-pairs is a minimal symmetric obstruction to the binary dichotomy.
The balanced ternary pair-pattern is used as an obstruction to the binary good/okay dichotomy, with the three output labels treated symmetrically.
The three-valued equal-pair obstruction is a regular nonbinary configuration used to show the binary good/okay dichotomy cannot directly generalize.
the three-valued balanced pair pattern is used as an obstruction showing the binary good/okay dichotomy proof fails for larger range
